source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
title: Ensure That IAM Users Are Not Assigned the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator Roles at Project Level

input_row.source: CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
input_row.title: Ensure That IAM Users Are Not Assigned the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator Roles at Project Level
input_row.description: It is recommended to assign the Service Account User (iam.serviceAccountUser) and Service Account Token Creator (iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator) roles to a user for a specific service account rather than assigning the role to a user at project level.
input_row.rationale: A service account is a special Google account that belongs to an application or a virtual machine (VM), instead of to an individual end-user. Application/VM-Instance uses the service account to call the service's Google API so that users aren't directly involved. In addition to being an identity, a service account is a resource that has IAM policies attached to it. These policies determine who can use the service account. Users with IAM roles to update the App Engine and Compute Engine instances (such as App Engine Deployer or Compute Instance Admin) can effectively run code as the service accounts used to run these instances, and indirectly gain access to all the resources for which the service accounts have access. Similarly, SSH access to a Compute Engine instance may also provide the ability to execute code as that instance/Service account. Based on business needs, there could be multiple user-managed service accounts configured for a project. Granting the iam.serviceAccountUser or iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator roles to a user for a project gives the user access to all service accounts in the project, including service accounts that may be created in the future. This can result in elevation of privileges by using service accounts and corresponding Compute Engine instances. In order to implement least privileges best practices, IAM users should not be assigned the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level. Instead, these roles should be assigned to a user for a specific service account, giving that user access to the service account. The Service Account User allows a user to bind a service account to a long-running job service, whereas the Service Account Token Creator role allows a user to directly impersonate (or assert) the identity of a service account. Impact: After revoking Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level from all impacted user account(s), these roles should be assigned to a user(s) for specific service account(s) according to business needs.
input_row.audit: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the IAM page in the GCP Console by visiting https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam 2. Click on the filter table text bar, Type Role: Service Account User. 3. Ensure no user is listed as a result of the filter. 4. Click on the filter table text bar, Type Role: Service Account Token Creator. 5. Ensure no user is listed as a result of the filter. From Google Cloud CLI To ensure IAM users are not assigned Service Account User role at the project level: gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json | jq '.bindings[].role' | grep "roles/iam.serviceAccountUser" gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json | jq '.bindings[].role' | grep "roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator" These commands should not return any output.
input_row.remediation: From Google Cloud Console 1. Go to the IAM page in the GCP Console by visiting: https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam. 2. Click on the filter table text bar. Type Role: Service Account User 3. Click the Delete Bin icon in front of the role Service Account User for every user listed as a result of a filter. 4. Click on the filter table text bar. Type Role: Service Account Token Creator 5. Click the Delete Bin icon in front of the role Service Account Token Creator for every user listed as a result of a filter. From Google Cloud CLI 1. Using a text editor, remove the bindings with the roles/iam.serviceAccountUser or roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator. For example, you can use the iam.json file shown below as follows: { "bindings": [ { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", ], "role": "roles/appengine.appViewer" }, { "members": [ "user:email1@gmail.com" ], "role": "roles/owner" }, { "members": [ "serviceAccount:our-project-123@appspot.gserviceaccount.com", "serviceAccount:123456789012-compute@developer.gserviceaccount.com" ], "role": "roles/editor" } ], "etag": "BwUjMhCsNvY=" } 2. Update the project's IAM policy: gcloud projects set-iam-policy PROJECT_ID iam.json Default Value: By default, users do not have the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator role assigned at project level. References: 1. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts 2. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-roles-to-service-accounts 3. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles 4. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-changing-revoking-access 5. https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam Additional Information: To assign the role roles/iam.serviceAccountUser or roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator to a user role on a service account instead of a project: 1. Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/projectselector/iam- admin/serviceaccounts 2. Select Target Project 3. Select target service account. Click Permissions on the top bar. It will open permission pane on right side of the page 4. Add desired members with Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator role.
input_row.impact: After revoking Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level from all impacted user account(s), these roles should be assigned to a user(s) for specific service account(s) according to business needs.
input_row.references: 1. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/service-accounts 2. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-roles-to-service-accounts 3. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/understanding-roles 4. https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/granting-changing-revoking-access 5. https://console.cloud.google.com/iam-admin/iam Additional Information: To assign the role roles/iam.serviceAccountUser or roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator to a user role on a service account instead of a project: 1. Go to https://console.cloud.google.com/projectselector/iam- admin/serviceaccounts 2. Select Target Project 3. Select target service account. Click Permissions on the top bar. It will open permission pane on right side of the page 4. Add desired members with Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator role.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**

The control regarding IAM Users not being assigned the Service Account User or Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level is entirely API-checkable. The audit instructions explicitly demonstrate the use of Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) commands to verify the assignment of these roles, confirming the ability to automate this check using GCP CLI.

**PROGRAM NAME:** gcp_iam_project_service_account_roles_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**

The automated program will perform the following checks:

- **GCP API Calls or Google Cloud SDK (gcloud) SDK Methods:**
  - Use `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID` to retrieve the IAM policy for the given project.
- **GCP CLI Commands:**
  - Use `jq` to filter the roles from the IAM policy JSON output: `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format json | jq '.bindings[].role'`
  - Check for the presence of `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`.

- **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
  - Enumerate all IAM bindings at the project level for each specified project.

- **Exact Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
  - Validate that no user “members” are assigned the `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` or `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` at the project level.

- **Clear Pass/Fail Criteria with Expected Values:**
  - **Pass:** The output does not include any user assignments for `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`.
  - **Fail:** Any user is found to have been granted `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` or `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` at the project level.

This approach ensures that the principle of least privilege is maintained by restricting elevated privileges from being broadly assigned to users across all service accounts in a project.
step2_review:
---

### 1. APPROACH VALIDATION: **AGREE**

- **Manual vs Automated:** The classification as "Automated" is correct. This control can be fully validated using GCP APIs or the `gcloud` CLI.
- **API/SDK/CLI Coverage:** The GCP IAM policy for a project can be retrieved using `gcloud projects get-iam-policy` or the [projects.getIamPolicy](https://cloud.google.com/iam/docs/reference/rest/v1/projects/getIamPolicy) API. The role assignments can be programmatically parsed for violations.
- **Other GCP Tools:** While GCP Config (Config Validator) or Security Command Center could also be leveraged, the CLI/API approach is sufficient and standard.

---

### 2. TECHNICAL ACCURACY: **Score: 8/10**

- **Automation Details:** The use of `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format=json` is correct. Parsing with `jq` is a common and valid approach.
- **Role Filtering:** The description correctly identifies the roles to check: `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` and `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`.
- **Member Type Filtering:** The assessment mentions "user assignments," but does not explicitly describe filtering for only `user:` members (vs. `serviceAccount:`, `group:`, etc.), which is important for accuracy.
- **Program Name:** `gcp_iam_project_service_account_roles_check` is descriptive, but could be more specific (see below).
- **Completeness:** The assessment does not mention checking for custom roles that might aggregate these permissions, nor does it clarify if group assignments are to be flagged (per CIS, typically only "user" members are in scope, but this should be explicit).
- **CLI Syntax:** The sample `jq` filter `.bindings[].role` only lists roles, but does not enumerate which members are assigned to those roles. The correct filter should examine both `role` and `members` fields.

---

### 3. IMPROVEMENTS

- **Member Filtering:** Explicitly filter for `user:` members in the `members` field of each binding for the specified roles. For example:
    ```bash
    gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format=json | \
      jq '.bindings[] | select((.role=="roles/iam.serviceAccountUser" or .role=="roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator")) | .members[] | select(startswith("user:"))'
    ```
- **Custom Roles:** Consider documenting that custom roles granting equivalent permissions are out of scope unless the policy requires their inclusion.
- **Group Assignments:** Clarify whether `group:` assignments should be flagged (per CIS, typically not, but should be explicit).
- **Program Name:** Recommend a more specific name, e.g.,  
  `gcp_iam_project_user_service_account_roles_assignment`
- **Output:** Recommend outputting both the violating user and the role for clear reporting.
- **Alternative Methods:** For large orgs, consider using [Cloud Asset Inventory](https://cloud.google.com/asset-inventory/docs/reference/rest/v1/assets/searchAllIamPolicies) for organization-wide policy analysis.

---

### 4. CONFIDENCE: **HIGH**

- The approach is fundamentally sound and leverages standard GCP API/CLI capabilities. Minor improvements would make it robust and production-ready.

---

### 5. FINAL RECOMMENDATION: **Modify**

- **Modify** the automation details to:
    - Explicitly filter for `user:` members.
    - Provide a more accurate `jq` filter as shown above.
    - Clarify scope regarding `group:` and custom roles.
    - Update the program name for clarity and specificity.

---

**Summary:**  
The assessment is correct in classifying this control as automatable and in using the GCP CLI/API. However, the technical implementation needs refinement to ensure only `user:` members are flagged, and the program name should be more descriptive. With these modifications, the control check will be robust and fully compliant with CIS intent.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:**  
Both AI opinions correctly identify that this control can be fully audited using GCP APIs or the gcloud CLI. The IAM policy for a project exposes all role bindings, including which members (users, groups, service accounts) are assigned the Service Account User and Service Account Token Creator roles at the project level. Parsing this policy for user assignments to these roles is straightforward and does not require manual inspection. This is a standard, automatable GCP compliance check.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:**  
gcp_iam_project_user_service_account_roles_assignment

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- GCP Resource/Service: IAM roles at the Project level
- API/CLI Method:  
  - `gcloud projects get-iam-policy PROJECT_ID --format=json`  
  - Or via API: `projects.getIamPolicy`
- Properties to Check:  
  - In the IAM policy bindings, look for:
    - `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser`
    - `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator`
  - For each binding, check if any member starts with `user:`
- Pass Criteria:  
  - No members of type `user:` are assigned either `roles/iam.serviceAccountUser` or `roles/iam.serviceAccountTokenCreator` at the project level.
- Fail Criteria:  
  - Any member of type `user:` is assigned either of these roles at the project level.

---

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:**  
Both AI opinions agreed on automation, but the second opinion noted the importance of filtering specifically for `user:` members. This is correct and is explicitly included in the final automation details for accuracy and completeness.

**SOURCE:** CIS GOOGLE CLOUD PLATFORM FOUNDATION BENCHMARK V4.0.0
